It has both macOS and Linux clients.When context compaction introduces a gap, I use the /total-recall skill to pull prior turns back into context and off it goes.The tool is free for personal use and has a source available local cloud option to sync convo histories across multiple machines.http://contextify.sh/docs Full agreement. I sincerely hope that reducing token is mostly a cost saving measure and not some roundabout way to get more token use. It still does not help. In my corp, bean counters rendered initially useful internal llm largely pointless as they severely limited context.I swear there is a executive convention out there sharing worst practices. Just use md files for working memory. No need for large context. LLMs get dumber as you put more in context, as it stretches their attention. Keeping context small is better for quality. Big context windows made everyone lazy about deciding what actually deserves to be in there... compaction makes it worse, it's lossy compression applied to everything at once so the details you actually needed probably get lost. I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problem... in my experience with conversational agents I clear the context every turn and reinject all the relevant info with a memory/context plugin I built myself, so small context stopped being a problem cos the model reads a few k of curated state instead of 200k of scrollback. Coding agents are harder and I haven't cracked that part, but in my opinion the real fix to the context problem is in this direction: a policy that keeps what's needed to finish the job or to do the next job and drops the rest. This can be achieved with a separate LLM specialized for exactly that. > I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problemSo did many NLP experts who worked on LSTM, GRU, etc. But transformers empirically won out and here we are.It will be interesting to see if future model architectures need to take this into account again.If humans are any indication, one thing we're still missing is efficient movement of information from short term to long term memory. Fine tuning in principle does something akin to this, but not efficiently. I primarily use Opus for my day to day and I /clear often. 1M context sound great but it quickly degrades once you start hitting the 50% mark. I usually just /clear once I hit between 30 and 40 percent context and get much better results from the model. Compaction doesn’t really help much in my experience, starting fresh and having the model front load its context from scratch works much better IME. I have multiple skills with a pile of markdown docs organized by feature to assist in that first load by telling it where to find relevant information for the task. Works great. I never felt that context size was any issue at all in codex. I don't know how their compaction works, but it just keeps on going as if it has no context limit at all. At least in my experience. > I never felt that context size was any issue at all in codex.I'm guessing you just started using codex, it suffered greatly from "model context size exceeded" errors early on, where compaction couldn't even recover from it, these errors just stopped happening maybe some month(s) ago.Nowadays it's a lot better though and I've don't get stuck in those anymore. Although I don't like the idea that they don't surface what goes into the "concise summary" afterwards, make it really hard to know if it actually got everything important or not.In general, codex seems to be moving into the way of hiding as much from the user as possible, and it wouldn't surprise me if they eventually start encrypting the entire session logs just like they did with agent>sub-agent prompts recently. Sad though as it's easily the best harness+model combo available today out of the ones I've tried. That has nothing to do with context window or the models at all; it's the compute capacity.Pretty much every single AI firm had the same issue when the new model came out, it's the result of everyone jumping onboard and consuming all available compute capacity to break it for days.Claude had the same issues when they released their newer models. > That has nothing to do with context window or the models at all; it's the compute capacity.Quite literally a harness feature/bug though, that it's unable to compact when reaching the max context (which seemingly both codex and Claude Code struggled with in the beginning), not something that will happen at every model release forever.Hasn't happen for at least the two last model releases in Codex, not sure why you'd imply that. Claude also had issues with this kind of thing for months early on, too, where it simply couldn't compact its own emissions.But definitely earlier GPT models suffered greatly as context got large, and the compaction itself in Codex was really crappy. That changed around January. For me, Codex regularly forgets to complete its last task when compaction occurs, especially when the last message I sent was right before compaction. It seems odd to me that Codex doesn't carry the plan file through context compaction? Claude does this, it re-reads it in full from disk I’ve noticed it having weird message dropping and replay in general, but the compaction boundary has been pretty solid. Most problems should be divide-and-conquerable into chunks that 300 vs 400k context is basically never an issue. A coding agent isn't an infinite chat. No matter how good compaction is, on some big projects it needs to read a lot of files. In my experience the first 200.000 tokens go FAST, but after that it slows down. Most of my Fable sessions don't go over 500.000 tokens, I don't need to compact once. But when I use Codex a single session has to compact over and over again. In my experience the only reason it needs to read a lot of files is if your agents.md is deficient. It should only need to read the file it's actually working on, maybe a few others. Everything else should be explained in documentation That's quite small for my workloads.I try to keep it under 200k but my DeepSeek and MiMo sessions can sometimes grow to 350k tokens when I try to squeeze one last iteration I compact.Can't OpenAI copy DeepSeek K/V cache tech (from published papers) to make it super cheap? Nobody does caching as well as DeepSeek, so I guess it's a big enough difference in the implementation to make it difficult.If you use Reasonix with DeepSeek it gets silly, as it is append-only to work with how caching works. It gets something like 97-98% cached tokens in a long session. It makes an already cheap model even cheaper. using open models and local AI via llamacpp, I'm compacting (via agent directed) between 55k-85k; going up to 120k is rare unless the agent is actually following a non trivial log tracing or some other thing that absolutely requires keeping large context.I've also tailored an agent system prompt with llamacpp reasoning budget and message that asks the agent to spawn subagents then compress to reduce context bloat. It's in opencode using the dynamic context pruning, so it's mostly keeping a vector&direction without having to bloat up.It mostly works to allow iteration into multiple sub components. On long sessions even with workflows and sub-agents I typically hit around 500-750k tokens in context (say 4-16 hours of work in a session, across multiple 5-hour limits) with Claude Code.I was going to explore paying for Codex since OpenAI seems to be a bit more generous with rate limits, but I'm now not sure - for the stuff I do limited context size would be a dealbreaker - basically lots and lots of documentation and guideline references, code review loops, documentation references and web searches, multi-repo exploration, lots of tool calls and so on.I might just upgrade to Anthropic's more expensive Max subscription or something to get more subsidized tokens. Even with plan files and the plan mode, it's like a slot machine after compacting the context, sometimes steps or other details just evaporate in thin air, the less of that I need to do the better. It's not that it can't work, it just doesn't work reliably enough not to be annoying.Luckily DeepSeek V4 Pro, GLM 5.2 and Kimi K3 don't seem to have those limits either - though DS is around Sonnet, GLM 5.2 feels a bit above Sonnet and only K3 only really is in the proper Opus ballpark that's good enough for me to work undisturbed, even if it seems to be slower. I didn't try long coding sessions for a while. I had really noticeable performance degradation with all models above 100-150k and always try to stay below those numbers. Good move given some experienced issues and compaction across the 5.6 range is closer to 5.4 than 5.5, i.e solid and reliable.Will say that 5.6-Sol is a minor bump in my benchmarks in most areas vs 5.5 but a severe regression in a few specific task focused on rearranging trees, addressing merge conflicts, etc. where the model to accomplish the task does not properly adhere to prompts in a way GPT-5 originally managed, not retaining parts of history in the way prompted despite specific instructions not to as that made the final completion easier…I am of the conservative and cautious opinion that no model should be able to run destructive tasks at all, I have seen every model do things that make me concerned enough to maintain that opinion and know my evals can’t catch everything. But for 5.6-Sol specifically, I’d caution everyone to reevaluate how you run the model, maybe take a few more precautions you tend to forgo.It is extremely capable as a reviewer and for extensive tasks, though for the later, the safety net I feel is required to be comfortable limits the utility. The code 5.6-Sol provides also still is a bit harder to parse in reviews.Release strategy wise, feel it’s have been smarter to release only Luna and Sol now, then Terra a few weeks of posttraining later, I simply cannot see a purpose for it in the current form given how well both Luna and Sol scale up and down respectively with reasoning. Two models from a lab at a time is also the limit I feel one can properly assess at a time. With purely quadratic attention, the cost of the token at 372K is 87% more than the token at 272K.There are attention mechanisms that help to mitigate this, but you can clearly see in the chart that Tibo posted that the attention mechanisms they are using are still scaling quadratically.This becomes an optimization problem for token t:min f[cost, quality loss] = compaction cost[t] + compaction quality loss[t] + token cost[t] On the one hand, compacting at 300k saves OpenAI 40%. That's great.On the other hand, $10 for 1M tokens still seems really high? It's not too hard to blow through that in an hour or two. I have over the last two months switched to OpenAI from Claude since it’s been vastly better for my cases. Wonder if I see any difference in the quality of the output with this change You can run ’codex debug models' into jq!I forget whether it's documented or not, and it is kind of annoying that that's how you find it, but it does tell you. Maybe useful for the future if nothing else. This is a shame, and hopefully it reverts soon. I switched from Claude to Codex just before the Fable release because Claude doesn't follow instructions and goes off the ranch too frequently. I really don't want to have to switch back to what I consider to be a fundamentally worse model. Not sure if this is still up to date (2023), but https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.03172 shows that performance degrades mostly in the middle of the context.Anecdotally I've been stuck in that situation of being at 400-500k tokens and "just one more prompt bro" will get the task done, and I appreciate not having to wait through a compaction. If anything, keeping the bloated context helps with accuracy at the expense of speed in these cases. I haven't used Codex in some time, but in Claude Code I force my context size down to 280k on purpose as in my experience your best work is done within the 30-250k~ range regardless of a models maximum context size. I have rule files that guides the agent towards my coding standards, code style, house rules etc. They alone cost 60-80k tokens, and they are the backbone of my system that prevents slop. Pre 1M context, I had to build complicated tooling to re-include the relevant docs to the context upon compaction, which relied on unstable transcription file format, which was a pain to maintain. With 1M context I deleted all of those. Nowadays most of my sessions uses 300-450k context.Another thing that's preventing me from trying Codex. (the other is @ referencing files not auto including them to the context)1M should be table stakes for frontier models at this point for programming. Try running your rule files through an LLM for optimization. 60k-80k tokens is massive.Funnily enough, most anti-slop skills I found are both way too verbose and miss some common slop constructs.I also reduced many rules from “When doing X, don’t do Y, but do Z.” Instead, the rule is “When doing X, do Z.” Fewer tokens and often works better.I had one critical rule I was maintaining about searching the codebase using a structural index/graph and not grep. Every time the agent missed it, I asked it how to improve the rules. Eventually, I asked the AI to review that rule file and it rewrote it to be 30% smaller, but, crucially, structured to be more understandable by the LLM.Another helpful thing was to ask AI to review my rules for things it can load on-demand when it works in that area. It’s optimized to death. Every word, every comma. I have a rule file for writing rule files.Problem stems from using my own abstractions instead of common frameworks.For a full stack session, backend + front end + docs about the part we are working on adds to that amount.I’ve accepted it because it results in code exactly the style I would’ve written. It’s a good tradeoff to cut off the slop. AI cannot tell you why it did something. If you ask it why it didn't do X even though your prompt said so, it can come up with some plausible sounding explanation, which might even be correct, but there's a fundamental impedance mismatch between giving you the most plausible sounding answer and actually grounding that in knowledge of how it generated that answer.TBF having a 60k personal style guide is also a bad idea, people get borderline AI-psychosed about how their special prompts are really steering the AI when in reality there is no way to evaluate this stuff. You invariably end up trying to define your style in terms of what it is and it isn't, it's like trying to define "red". "follow the style of my 5 most recent PRs" works pretty well. Another interesting change in that commit is the addition of this section to the system prompt: Before taking a destructive action: - Make sure the action is clearly within the user's request. - Resolve the exact targets with read-only checks when necessary. - Do not use `$HOME`, `~`, `/`, a workspace root, or another broad directory as the target of a recursive or destructive command Looks like the fix for this bug where Codex would occasionally accidentally delete your entire home directory: https://twitter.com/thsottiaux/status/2077630111499882637 https://github.com/tekacs/fast-rmI've overridden my rm with this, which I threw together for fast-deletes of things like Rust target/ directories, and after seeing the GPT horror story, I taught it to flatly reject deletions directly under `/` and under home directories, with a message printing the path that it's trying to delete.Not exactly a perfect mitigation, but given that the stated risk was the model mistakenly using the wrong $HOME, it seems like a reasonable safety. I should probably make it use an even scarier rejection notice, though.I also... have backups. This happened to me one time. On windows though, deleting C:/ lmao. The direction I'm heading now is a better harness, i.e. isolating codex at a container level with dedicated workspaces / mounts etc. I'm building something at the moment that serves my needs. This was done because they were charging the 100%/50% premium for long-context over 272k to Codex users, even though they set 372k as the default. It cost people a LOT of money. Codex is open source. You can build it yourself. You don't have to use upstream's parameter choices, compaction strategies, subagent heuristics, or whatever. Strange how people act like tweaks to these parameters are mandates when they're really just suggestions relayed to you via git.For example: upstream recently changed the effort level hotkeys so M-. would stop at xhigh, not max. If you want max, upstream, you go to the /model menu. I didn't like this change, so I undid it locally. Easy peasy. Not true. Try to increase the context window and you will be greeted with an error when you reach the real limit enforced server-side. The models themselves support up to 1M. You are just charged more for all context over 400k. The 272k limit is just a client limitation to ensure you can never go over 400k since the maximum output size of the model is 128k. I am wondering why the codex repo is open source ? Can they afford it to be open source in the longer run ? Not much point making it closed source at this point, coding agent harnesses are trivial to reverse-engineer.Being open source was also a useful community lever they could pull when they were trying to catch up with Claude Code. Codex compaction is really quite good. Smaller context doesn't really harm me.I had a /goal running last night for 9.5 hours straight while I slept. When I woke up in the morning it was fully on task and focused.Write up a detailed design doc. Build a decent AGENTS.md, and write up a good prompt or /goal.Long context can be more of a curse than a benefit sometimes anyways. If ChatGPT 5.6 is so good why do they continue to have the same motherfucking cache and usage issues that they were struggling with when 5.2 is new? Could not pay me to use this shit. Enjoy your reset bingo That is a good point actually (this is how we at Hugston measure tokens, in bytes). 270k tokens should be around ~1100kb or 1 MB, so not really enough for a serious project. Context size cuts like this are usually a cost/latency tradeoff rather than a capability one - serving a smaller window is cheaper and keeps latency in check, and most sessions probably don't need anywhere near 372k tokens anyway. Curious if this affects people who were actually relying on the larger window for big codebases. We'll have to wait because Tibo (OpenAI) said this was a temporary measure and it'd go back to 372k soon.The issue was more specific to higher token burn rates, not latency. You just need good compaction and a memory system with decent project layout and documentation. If you don't have that 1M tokens won't really save you either.

blocks.html_en:
I know a lot of people like to say that compaction makes this moot, but the level of detail you lose across compaction is wildly too much for most things that I do, unfortunately.

Perhaps if your plans don't have as much detail, or if you're not, for example, having a discussion with a lot of nitty-gritty then it's fine?

The lack of long context is the main reason that I still end up using Anthropic.

The worst is when you need it to hold for example a number of papers in its head, or large and complex materials that it needs full resolution on and your context window ends up being perennially at 16%. You have about five minutes of conversation and it compacts and then you have to wait for it to read that again, get to 16%... and repeat.

372 was not perfect, but it was so much better and a godsend. It turned that 12 to 20% into more like 40%.

sentences.en: I know a lot of people like to say that compaction makes this moot, but the level of detail you lose across compaction is wildly too much for most things that I do, unfortunately.Perhaps if your plans don't have as much detail, or if you're not, for example, having a discussion with a lot of nitty-gritty then it's fine?The lack of long context is the main reason that I still end up using Anthropic.The worst is when you need it to hold for example a number of papers in its head, or large and complex materials that it needs full resolution on and your context window ends up being perennially at 16%.
sentences.zh: 我知道很多人喜欢说压缩使这个毫无意义，但不幸的是，对于我所做的大多数事情来说，压缩过程中丢失的细节水平太多了。也许如果你的计划没有那么多细节，或者如果你没有与很多细节进行讨论，那么没关系？缺乏长上下文是我最终仍然使用 Anthropic 的主要原因。最糟糕的是当你需要它来保存大量论文时在它的头脑中，或者需要完全分辨率的大型复杂材料中，你的上下文窗口最终会始终保持在 16%。

sentences.en: You have about five minutes of conversation and it compacts and then you have to wait for it to read that again, get to 16%...
sentences.zh: 你有大约五分钟的对话，内容很紧凑，然后你必须等待它再次阅读，达到 16%...

sentences.en: and repeat.372 was not perfect, but it was so much better and a godsend.
sentences.zh: 和repeat.372并不完美，但它好多了，是天赐之物。

sentences.en: It turned that 12 to 20% into more like 40%.
sentences.zh: 它把 12% 到 20% 变成了 40% 左右。

blocks.html_en: All depends on context coherence. E.g. in Claude Code with the "1M context" models I am reluctant to push past 30%. So if that Codex 272K is 100% coherent than it is less of a difference. Still, even then the "hard fail" boundary has moved. Longer running agentic processes will surely run into this more often.

sentences.en: All depends on context coherence.
sentences.zh: 一切都取决于上下文的连贯性。

sentences.en: E.g.
sentences.zh: 例如。

sentences.en: in Claude Code with the "1M context" models I am reluctant to push past 30%.
sentences.zh: 在 Claude Code 的“1M context”模型中，我不愿意超过 30%。

sentences.en: So if that Codex 272K is 100% coherent than it is less of a difference.
sentences.zh: 因此，如果 Codex 272K 100% 一致，那么差异就较小。

sentences.en: Still, even then the "hard fail" boundary has moved.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，“硬失败”的界限已经发生了变化。

sentences.en: Longer running agentic processes will surely run into this more often.
sentences.zh: 运行时间较长的代理进程肯定会更频繁地遇到这种情况。

blocks.html_en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction and no way to go back in the conversation history to before a compact makes codex a no-go for me on any codebase > 5kloc. Even worse it seems to fire randomly at 10-20% of context left. So really you have 80% of 272k as real usable context. Compaction kills my sessions, it hallucinates and is worse than starting fresh. I've had enough times screaming at my computer when it burns tokens on a large codebase, gets to 15%, auto-compacts, and hallucinates so bad it has to read the entire codebase agin, gets to 15%, auto-compacts....

sentences.en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction and no way to go back in the conversation history to before a compact makes codex a no-go for me on any codebase > 5kloc.
sentences.zh: 事实上，没有办法禁用自动压缩，也没有办法在对话历史中返回压缩之前的状态，这使得 Codex 对我来说在任何大于 5kloc 的代码库上都是不行的。

sentences.en: Even worse it seems to fire randomly at 10-20% of context left.
sentences.zh: 更糟糕的是，它似乎在剩下 10-20% 的上下文时随机触发。

sentences.en: So really you have 80% of 272k as real usable context.
sentences.zh: 所以实际上 272k 中的 80% 是真正可用的上下文。

sentences.en: Compaction kills my sessions, it hallucinates and is worse than starting fresh.
sentences.zh: 压缩会毁掉我的训练，它会产生幻觉，而且比重新开始更糟糕。

blocks.html_en:
> you can use /tree to go back before the compaction

This is really a killer feature in my opinion. I'm currently working on pi brains extension that is designed to solve the compaction issue I have, which is, I don't know what the AI knows after compaction.

https://gitsense.com/screenshots/pi-inspect-overview.png

The screenshot shot above contains what I call a compaction capsule which contains all the messages/events at the point of compaction. The "Files" section in the right side panel shows me how many files were read/modified since compaction.

After compacting, I can easily tell what Pi use to know and use `/tree` to go back in time.

I still wouldn't compact more than 5 times before starting a new session since compaction messages persists in Pi.

sentences.en: > you can use /tree to go back before the compactionThis is really a killer feature in my opinion.
sentences.zh: > 你可以使用 /tree 在压缩之前返回，在我看来这确实是一个杀手级功能。

sentences.en: I'm currently working on pi brains extension that is designed to solve the compaction issue I have, which is, I don't know what the AI knows after compaction.https://gitsense.com/screenshots/pi-inspect-overview.pngThe screenshot shot above contains what I call a compaction capsule which contains all the messages/events at the point of compaction.
sentences.zh: 我目前正在开发 pi Brains 扩展，旨在解决我遇到的压缩问题，也就是说，我不知道压缩后人工智能知道什么。https://gitsense.com/screenshots/pi-inspect-overview.png 上面的屏幕截图包含我所说的压缩胶囊，其中包含压缩时的所有消息/事件。

sentences.en: The "Files" section in the right side panel shows me how many files were read/modified since compaction.After compacting, I can easily tell what Pi use to know and use `/tree` to go back in time.I still wouldn't compact more than 5 times before starting a new session since compaction messages persists in Pi.
sentences.zh: 右侧面板中的“文件”部分显示了自压缩以来读取/修改了多少文件。压缩后，我可以轻松地知道 Pi 使用什么来了解并使用 `/tree` 及时返回。在开始新会话之前，我仍然不会压缩超过 5 次，因为压缩消息仍然存在于 Pi 中。

blocks.html_en:
So the practical workaround is to set model_auto_compact_token_limit very near, but still below, the model’s context window. This postpones compaction rather than disabling it.

config.toml

  model_context_window = 27000
  model_auto_compact_token_limit = 17000
  model_auto_compact_token_limit_scope = "total"

sentences.en: So the practical workaround is to set model_auto_compact_token_limit very near, but still below, the model’s context window.
sentences.zh: 因此，实际的解决方法是将 model_auto_compact_token_limit 设置为非常接近模型的上下文窗口，但仍低于模型的上下文窗口。

> The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction and no way to go back in the conversation history to before a compact makes codex a no-go for me

Same. I've told @tibo many times about these issues but apparently OpenAI's focus is on features that buy them the most users ASAP instead of increasing harness quality.

blocks.html_en:
I did find a strategy, thank you very much, and it was easy:

  npm uninstall -g @openai/codex && curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

This was the final straw that got me to downgrade my OpenAI subscription and move the bulk of my spending to Anthropic and Claude code.

OpenAI devs, if you're reading, this is how you lost a customer.

blocks.html_en:
Have you observed any decrease in performance at context higher than 200k?

That used to be one of the major issues why clearing or compacting context when the context nears 150 to 170k is usually preferred.

blocks.html_en:
Not really, on the project I'm working on right now most of my longer prompts seems to consume somewhere between 300k and 400k tokens (input + output) and I find that both Opus 4.8 and Fable 5 don't seem to get lost with that much context. At least the results still look good to me.

I don't think I've ever pushed past 500k though, it may well fall apart at the higher end of what is officially supported.

blocks.html_en:
I like to actually have hierarchical plans.

Start with the big idea. Pin down the product manager-level description, and maybe some details about which features are in or out, how the phased design/roadmap will work, maybe what the basic tech stack will be.

That document gets saved, and has the big picture context.

Next, we go a little deeper, flesh out schemas, APIs, a little more about what the code should look like, the overall testing strategy. This document gets saved, and the AI can reference it for context.

The next level is a very specific implementation plan. Think individual small JIRA tickets in an epic. The AI writes this based on previous context. I make sure each individual step has very explicit instructions about branch names, which branch is branched from where, the testing and documentation that will be done for that step. It should be obvious from the higher level documents, but that helps keep the AI to only worrying about the local document in its context window.

Then have a DAG of stacked pull requests designed for subagents to work through.

All of this keeps the context window down (as well as costs down, and accuracy up) and works super well, and I can get a tremendous amount of code that is easy to review through stacked PRs. Invariably I will want to fix earlier ones then percolate that down the stack, so I have some skills for that.

sentences.en: I like to actually have hierarchical plans.Start with the big idea.
sentences.zh: 我喜欢实际上有分层计划。从大想法开始。

sentences.en: Pin down the product manager-level description, and maybe some details about which features are in or out, how the phased design/roadmap will work, maybe what the basic tech stack will be.That document gets saved, and has the big picture context.Next, we go a little deeper, flesh out schemas, APIs, a little more about what the code should look like, the overall testing strategy.
sentences.zh: 确定产品经理级别的描述，也许还有一些关于哪些功能已加入或取消的细节，分阶段设计/路线图将如何工作，也许基本技术堆栈是什么。该文档被保存，并具有大背景。接下来，我们更深入一些，充实模式、API，更多关于代码应该是什么样子的，整体测试策略。

sentences.en: This document gets saved, and the AI can reference it for context.The next level is a very specific implementation plan.
sentences.zh: 该文档被保存，人工智能可以参考它的上下文。下一个级别是一个非常具体的实施计划。

sentences.en: Think individual small JIRA tickets in an epic.
sentences.zh: 想象一下史诗中的个人小 JIRA 门票。

sentences.en: The AI writes this based on previous context.
sentences.zh: AI 根据之前的上下文编写此内容。

sentences.en: I make sure each individual step has very explicit instructions about branch names, which branch is branched from where, the testing and documentation that will be done for that step.
sentences.zh: 我确保每个单独的步骤都有关于分支名称、哪个分支从哪里分支、该步骤将完成的测试和文档的非常明确的说明。

sentences.en: It should be obvious from the higher level documents, but that helps keep the AI to only worrying about the local document in its context window.Then have a DAG of stacked pull requests designed for subagents to work through.All of this keeps the context window down (as well as costs down, and accuracy up) and works super well, and I can get a tremendous amount of code that is easy to review through stacked PRs.
sentences.zh: 从更高级别的文档中这一点应该是显而易见的，但这有助于让人工智能只关心其上下文窗口中的本地文档。然后有一个为子代理设计的堆叠拉取请求的 DAG，以供子代理处理。所有这些都使上下文窗口保持较小（以及成本下降和准确性提高）并且工作得非常好，并且我可以获得大量易于​​通过堆叠 PR 进行审查的代码。

sentences.en: Invariably I will want to fix earlier ones then percolate that down the stack, so I have some skills for that.
sentences.zh: 我总是想要修复早期的问题，然后将其渗透到堆栈中，所以我有一些这方面的技能。

sentences.zh: 确定产品经理级别的描述，也许还有一些关于哪些功能已加入或取消的细节，分阶段设计/路线图将如何工作，也许基本技术堆栈是什么。

blocks.html_en: That document gets saved, and has the big picture context.

sentences.en: That document gets saved, and has the big picture context.
sentences.zh: 该文档被保存，并具有全局背景。

blocks.html_en: Next, we go a little deeper, flesh out schemas, APIs, a little more about what the code should look like, the overall testing strategy. This document gets saved, and the AI can reference it for context.

sentences.en: Next, we go a little deeper, flesh out schemas, APIs, a little more about what the code should look like, the overall testing strategy.
sentences.zh: 接下来，我们更深入一些，充实模式、API，更多地了解代码应该是什么样子，以及整体测试策略。

sentences.en: This document gets saved, and the AI can reference it for context.
sentences.zh: 该文档被保存，人工智能可以参考它作为上下文。

blocks.html_en: The next level is a very specific implementation plan. Think individual small JIRA tickets in an epic. The AI writes this based on previous context. I make sure each individual step has very explicit instructions about branch names, which branch is branched from where, the testing and documentation that will be done for that step. It should be obvious from the higher level documents, but that helps keep the AI to only worrying about the local document in its context window.

sentences.en: The next level is a very specific implementation plan.
sentences.zh: 下一个层次是非常具体的实施计划。

sentences.en: Think individual small JIRA tickets in an epic.
sentences.zh: 想象一下史诗中的个人小 JIRA 门票。

sentences.en: The AI writes this based on previous context.
sentences.zh: AI 根据之前的上下文编写此内容。

sentences.en: I make sure each individual step has very explicit instructions about branch names, which branch is branched from where, the testing and documentation that will be done for that step.
sentences.zh: 我确保每个单独的步骤都有关于分支名称、哪个分支从哪里分支、该步骤将完成的测试和文档的非常明确的说明。

sentences.en: It should be obvious from the higher level documents, but that helps keep the AI to only worrying about the local document in its context window.
sentences.zh: 从更高级别的文档来看，这一点应该是显而易见的，但这有助于让 AI 只关心其上下文窗口中的本地文档。

blocks.html_en: Then have a DAG of stacked pull requests designed for subagents to work through.

sentences.en: Then have a DAG of stacked pull requests designed for subagents to work through.
sentences.zh: 然后设计一个由堆叠拉取请求组成的 DAG，供子代理处理。

blocks.html_en: All of this keeps the context window down (as well as costs down, and accuracy up) and works super well, and I can get a tremendous amount of code that is easy to review through stacked PRs. Invariably I will want to fix earlier ones then percolate that down the stack, so I have some skills for that.

sentences.en: All of this keeps the context window down (as well as costs down, and accuracy up) and works super well, and I can get a tremendous amount of code that is easy to review through stacked PRs.
sentences.zh: 所有这些都保持了上下文窗口的缩小（以及成本的降低和准确性的提高）并且工作得非常好，而且我可以获得大量的代码，这些代码很容易通过堆积的 PR 进行审查。

sentences.en: Invariably I will want to fix earlier ones then percolate that down the stack, so I have some skills for that.
sentences.zh: 我总是想要修复早期的问题，然后将其渗透到堆栈中，所以我有一些这方面的技能。

blocks.html_en: I try to do similar. I'll even break down parts of the plan into separate docs and have it solely refer to them one by one as needed with /clear between. It's a more manual process but not the worst. I could probably even whip up some kind of skill to automate it somewhat with subagents or similar.

sentences.zh: 我什至可能会掌握某种技能，通过子代理或类似的工具将其自动化。

blocks.html_en: You can just tell your main agents "I have these implementation docs in <DIRECTORY>, please spawn parallel subagents to implement all of them" and I generally find that works, and what's great is it's in parallel too so takes less wall clock time (even if there is slight token duplication from having the main agent and the subagent both read the prompt/doc, but overall I think it is less usage of tokens unless you go crazy with /clear).

sentences.en: You can just tell your main agents "I have these implementation docs in <DIRECTORY>, please spawn parallel subagents to implement all of them" and I generally find that works, and what's great is it's in parallel too so takes less wall clock time (even if there is slight token duplication from having the main agent and the subagent both read the prompt/doc, but overall I think it is less usage of tokens unless you go crazy with /clear).
sentences.zh: 您可以告诉您的主要代理“我在 <DIRECTORY> 中有这些实现文档，请生成并行子代理来实现所有这些文档”，我通常发现这是可行的，而且很棒的是它也是并行的，因此需要更少的挂钟时间（即使主代理和子代理都读取提示/文档会导致轻微的令牌重复，但总的来说，我认为除非您对 /clear 感到疯狂，否则令牌的使用会减少）。

blocks.html_en:
So far I have three different folders in my project for three different types of md documents that I keep creating and revising together with the agent: reports, plans, and reviews

Every large feature has at least one of each file created for it in that order. I start by analysing stuff and making reports that might also contain design thinking and decisions. Once I'm satisfied we have properly analysed and thought though whatever it is we're working on then I might make a plan or just jump into implementation depending on how complex the issue is. And then I might have zero or multiple reviews of different kinds by different agents depending on how much verification I think the issue warrants.

This is my super simple worse-is-better development process. I make up what is needed on the spot.

sentences.en: So far I have three different folders in my project for three different types of md documents that I keep creating and revising together with the agent: reports, plans, and reviewsEvery large feature has at least one of each file created for it in that order.
sentences.zh: 到目前为止，我的项目中有三个不同的文件夹，用于存放三种不同类型的 md 文档，我不断与代理一起创建和修改这些文档：报告、计划和评论。每个大型功能都至少有一个按顺序为其创建的每个文件。

sentences.en: I start by analysing stuff and making reports that might also contain design thinking and decisions.
sentences.zh: 我首先分析东西并制作可能还包含设计思维和决策的报告。

sentences.en: Once I'm satisfied we have properly analysed and thought though whatever it is we're working on then I might make a plan or just jump into implementation depending on how complex the issue is.
sentences.zh: 一旦我满意，我们已经正确分析和思考了无论我们正在做什么，那么我可能会根据问题的复杂程度制定计划或直接实施。

sentences.en: And then I might have zero or multiple reviews of different kinds by different agents depending on how much verification I think the issue warrants.This is my super simple worse-is-better development process.
sentences.zh: 然后，我可能会收到不同代理的零次或多次不同类型的评论，具体取决于我认为该问题需要多少验证。这是我的超级简单的“更差就是更好”的开发过程。

sentences.en: I make up what is needed on the spot.
sentences.zh: 需要的东西我当场补上。

However much detail gets put into the plan, it still doesn't help if part of what the model needs to understand is the fine-grained / perfect detail of a large surface area.

blocks.html_en: I did calculations on the prefix cache effect on costs of sessions where I used it and found that the removal of tokens from context had a much bigger effect on reducing costs than cache busting had on increasing them. I should re-do that and publish it.

sentences.en: I did calculations on the prefix cache effect on costs of sessions where I used it and found that the removal of tokens from context had a much bigger effect on reducing costs than cache busting had on increasing them.
sentences.zh: 我计算了前缀缓存对使用它的会话成本的影响，发现从上下文中删除令牌对降低成本的影响比缓存清除对增加成本的影响要大得多。

sentences.en: I should re-do that and publish it.
sentences.zh: 我应该重新做并发布它。

blocks.html_en:
Yes the idea is cool but this could really hammer usage, especially just leaving it up to the agent to decide when to do it. I'm not surprised though, considering the github account is named "Vibecodelicious" and became active in December.

Poking around in the repo the whole implementation is an unsupervised LLM fever-dream.

sentences.en: Yes the idea is cool but this could really hammer usage, especially just leaving it up to the agent to decide when to do it.
sentences.zh: 是的，这个想法很酷，但这确实会影响使用，特别是让代理来决定何时执行。

sentences.en: I'm not surprised though, considering the github account is named "Vibecodelicious" and became active in December.Poking around in the repo the whole implementation is an unsupervised LLM fever-dream.

blocks.html_en:
It depends on what you mean by "unsupervised" - I've been strictly working through the agent, through specs the entire time, but it's been very supervised, I just leave the mistakes in-place and have it work from there.

However at this point it can completely maintain itself. When a new version of Claude or OpenCode is released, it updates itself to work on the latest version. It can also add new implementations for harnesses pretty reliably. It's actually pretty fun to watch it at this point. "Make this work on Hermes agent and message me when you're done" and an hour later or so, I can go play with it in Hermes.

sentences.en: It depends on what you mean by "unsupervised" - I've been strictly working through the agent, through specs the entire time, but it's been very supervised, I just leave the mistakes in-place and have it work from there.However at this point it can completely maintain itself.
sentences.zh: 这取决于你所说的“无人监督”是什么意思——我一直在严格地通过代理、通过规范进行工作，但它受到了严格的监督，我只是把错误留在原处，然后让它从那里开始工作。但是在这一点上它可以完全维护自己。

sentences.en: When a new version of Claude or OpenCode is released, it updates itself to work on the latest version.
sentences.zh: 当 Claude 或 OpenCode 的新版本发布时，它会自行更新以在最新版本上工作。

sentences.en: It can also add new implementations for harnesses pretty reliably.
sentences.zh: 它还可以非常可靠地为线束添加新的实现。

sentences.en: It's actually pretty fun to watch it at this point.
sentences.zh: 此时观看它实际上非常有趣。

sentences.en: "Make this work on Hermes agent and message me when you're done" and an hour later or so, I can go play with it in Hermes.
sentences.zh: “在 Hermes 代理上完成这项工作，完成后给我发消息”，大约一个小时后，我就可以在 Hermes 中玩它了。

blocks.html_en:
When I've asked Codex agents about things that were in their context window, they've never – to my experience – been able to actually retrieve something from before compaction when using the proprietary compaction endpoint. Instead, they've had to consult their actual transcript.

So... at least as of a week ago or so, I don't believe so.

sentences.en: Instead, they've had to consult their actual transcript.So...
sentences.zh: 相反，他们必须查阅他们的实际成绩单。所以......

sentences.en: at least as of a week ago or so, I don't believe so.
sentences.zh: 至少在一周前左右，我不这么认为。

blocks.html_en: Instead of compaction, I very aggressively do everything with teams of subagents. The main agent keeps the high level context, and all of the nitty gritty detail and code details not needed by the main agent never even make it into the context.

sentences.en: Instead of compaction, I very aggressively do everything with teams of subagents.
sentences.zh: 我没有进行压缩，而是非常积极地与子代理团队一起完成所有事情。

sentences.en: The main agent keeps the high level context, and all of the nitty gritty detail and code details not needed by the main agent never even make it into the context.
sentences.zh: 主代理保留高级上下文，主代理不需要的所有细节和代码细节甚至永远不会进入上下文。

blocks.html_en:
This used to be really effective and cheap, now it lights my quota on fire, even after I fix the config and restore smaller subagents instead of like-agents

Multiple (slow) conversations seems to be the efficient path for me the past week or so

blocks.html_en: The solution isn’t to stuff everything it needs to know into the context, it is to have it write out what it needs to know for every part. It should write out plans, infrastructure decisions, etc, and then use those in future work as needed.

sentences.en: It should write out plans, infrastructure decisions, etc, and then use those in future work as needed.
sentences.zh: 它应该写出计划、基础设施决策等，然后根据需要在未来的工作中使用它们。

blocks.html_en: I suffered this same problem with Anthropic, ironically. Mainly with reverse engineering things. That would usually end up requiring a ton of context it had to remember, and then compaction would kick in and it would just forget half of it (that half being really important too), and even switching to Claude Code and having it document everything wouldn't really fix the problem. It was honestly quite sad because I've usually gotten better results with it, but it falls a part the moment you try doing something like that that's really complicated and requires a ton of different attempts to get it right.

sentences.en: I suffered this same problem with Anthropic, ironically.
sentences.zh: 讽刺的是，我在 Anthropic 上也遇到了同样的问题。

sentences.en: Mainly with reverse engineering things.
sentences.zh: 主要是逆向工程的东西。

sentences.en: That would usually end up requiring a ton of context it had to remember, and then compaction would kick in and it would just forget half of it (that half being really important too), and even switching to Claude Code and having it document everything wouldn't really fix the problem.
sentences.zh: 这通常最终需要它必须记住大量的上下文，然后压缩就会开始，它只会忘记其中的一半（那一半也非常重要），甚至切换到 Claude Code 并让它记录所有内容并不能真正解决问题。

sentences.en: It was honestly quite sad because I've usually gotten better results with it, but it falls a part the moment you try doing something like that that's really complicated and requires a ton of different attempts to get it right.
sentences.zh: 老实说，这很令人伤心，因为我通常会得到更好的结果，但当你尝试做这样的事情时，它就会成为一部分，这真的很复杂，需要大量不同的尝试才能得到正确的结果。

blocks.html_en:
For those kind of tasks, I do hierarchical planning, and then parts of the plan can focus one agent on keeping a smaller amount of pre-determined context when orchestrating.

Think: A VP of engineering has some super high level goal: "build a new product to beat our competitor in this new market". It then filters down and various levels of reports build more and more detailed yet more narrow pieces of planning/orchestration. They don't make it work by keeping all of the context in their head and micromanaging.

sentences.en: For those kind of tasks, I do hierarchical planning, and then parts of the plan can focus one agent on keeping a smaller amount of pre-determined context when orchestrating.Think: A VP of engineering has some super high level goal: "build a new product to beat our competitor in this new market".
sentences.zh: 对于这类任务，我会进行分层规划，然后计划的一部分可以集中于一个代理在编排时保留少量的预先确定的上下文。想想：工程副总裁有一些超高级别的目标：“开发一款新产品，在这个新市场上击败我们的竞争对手”。

sentences.en: It then filters down and various levels of reports build more and more detailed yet more narrow pieces of planning/orchestration.
sentences.zh: 然后，它会进行过滤，各个级别的报告会构建越来越详细但更狭窄的规划/编排部分。

sentences.en: They don't make it work by keeping all of the context in their head and micromanaging.
sentences.zh: 他们不会通过将所有背景都记在脑子里并进行微观管理来使其发挥作用。

blocks.html_en: Yes, sometimes planning spans sessions too but I have even that broken down into stages where we start with the architecture and research, then save that, then define the milestones, then break down into the individual issues that the less sophisticated models can implement in a single session.

sentences.en: Yes, sometimes planning spans sessions too but I have even that broken down into stages where we start with the architecture and research, then save that, then define the milestones, then break down into the individual issues that the less sophisticated models can implement in a single session.
sentences.zh: 是的，有时规划也跨越会议，但我什至将其分解为几个阶段，我们从架构和研究开始，然后保存，然后定义里程碑，然后分解为不太复杂的模型可以在单个会议中实现的各个问题。

blocks.html_en:
Pay attention to what it forgets, and start telling it to proactively note down those things into nominated files while it works, e.g. indexing topics covered by each chapter/section/page of each paper/material. GPT-5.6 is really good at finding context again, if given the smallest hint where to start looking.

You can also ask it to read your past sessions, find places where it was wrong after compaction, and figure out a strategy to persist the most important summary information.

I have several Hermes threads that have each had >10 compactions with a 200k context limit, and with the right instructions on note-taking, they require at most a "double-check that against our past decision records" to be put back on track after a slip-up.

sentences.en: Pay attention to what it forgets, and start telling it to proactively note down those things into nominated files while it works, e.g. indexing topics covered by each chapter/section/page of each paper/material.
sentences.zh: 注意它忘记的内容，并开始告诉它在工作时主动将这些内容记录到指定文件中，例如索引每篇论文/材料的每一章/节/页所涵盖的主题。

sentences.en: GPT-5.6 is really good at finding context again, if given the smallest hint where to start looking.You can also ask it to read your past sessions, find places where it was wrong after compaction, and figure out a strategy to persist the most important summary information.I have several Hermes threads that have each had >10 compactions with a 200k context limit, and with the right instructions on note-taking, they require at most a "double-check that against our past decision records" to be put back on track after a slip-up.
sentences.zh: 如果给出从哪里开始查找的最小提示，GPT-5.6 确实很擅长再次查找上下文。您还可以要求它读取过去的会话，找到压缩后出错的地方，并找出保留最重要摘要信息的策略。我有几个 Hermes 线程，每个线程都有超过 10 次压缩，上下文限制为 200k，并且有了正确的笔记说明，它们最多需要“根据我们过去的决策记录进行双重检查”，以便在失误。

sentences.zh: 注意它忘记的内容，并开始告诉它在工作时主动将这些内容记录到指定文件中，例如索引每篇论文/材料的每一章/节/页所涵盖的主题。

sentences.en: GPT-5.6 is really good at finding context again, if given the smallest hint where to start looking.You can also ask it to read your past sessions, find places where it was wrong after compaction, and figure out a strategy to persist the most important summary information.I have several Hermes threads that have each had >10 compactions with a 200k context limit, and with the right instructions on note-taking, they require at most a "double-check that against our past decision records" to be put back on track after a slip-up.
sentences.zh: 如果给出从哪里开始查找的最小提示，GPT-5.6 确实很擅长再次查找上下文。您还可以要求它读取过去的会话，找到压缩后出错的地方，并找出保留最重要摘要信息的策略。我有几个 Hermes 线程，每个线程都有超过 10 次压缩，上下文限制为 200k，并且有了正确的笔记说明，它们最多需要“根据我们过去的决策记录进行双重检查”，以便在失误。

blocks.html_en: I have several Hermes threads that have each had >10 compactions with a 200k context limit, and with the right instructions on note-taking, they require at most a "double-check that against our past decision records" to be put back on track after a slip-up.

sentences.en: I have several Hermes threads that have each had >10 compactions with a 200k context limit, and with the right instructions on note-taking, they require at most a "double-check that against our past decision records" to be put back on track after a slip-up.

blocks.html_en: I think I've seen the opposite end of this as well: I get the most value from any LLM when I ask it one very targeted question with a followup. That isn't to say that I don't have a few long engineering discussions that are helpful, but most are very to the point. It helps that I have decades of experience at this point and effectively know what to ask to narrow the scope quickly.

blocks.html_en: sometimes compaction is a death knell and other times it's totally fine. it really just depends on whether the thing you were doing at the very end has high correlation with stuff from early on in the session, or if there was an obvious ~break s.t. you were just on some specific subtask. the situation has clearly gotten a lot better than even 6 months ago-- i think openai handles compaction better in my own experience. but it's exactly the kind of thing that yegge was trying to get at with gastown-- i used to run every session s.t. i could re-animate it every time the compaction boundary came... now, when the compaction boundary comes i am so pot committed that i just hold my breath.

sentences.en: sometimes compaction is a death knell and other times it's totally fine.
sentences.zh: 有时压实是丧钟，有时又完全没问题。

sentences.en: it really just depends on whether the thing you were doing at the very end has high correlation with stuff from early on in the session, or if there was an obvious ~break s.t.
sentences.zh: 这实际上只取决于你最后所做的事情是否与会议早期的事情有很高的相关性，或者是否有明显的~break s.t.。

sentences.en: you were just on some specific subtask.
sentences.zh: 你只是在执行一些特定的子任务。

sentences.en: the situation has clearly gotten a lot better than even 6 months ago-- i think openai handles compaction better in my own experience.
sentences.zh: 情况显然比 6 个月前好多了——根据我自己的经验，我认为 openai 可以更好地处理压缩。

sentences.en: but it's exactly the kind of thing that yegge was trying to get at with gastown-- i used to run every session s.t.
sentences.zh: 但这正是 yegge 试图通过 Gastown 实现的目标——我过去常常运行每个会话。

sentences.en: i could re-animate it every time the compaction boundary came...
sentences.zh: 每次压缩边界到来时我都可以重新设置动画......

sentences.en: now, when the compaction boundary comes i am so pot committed that i just hold my breath.
sentences.zh: 现在，当压实边界到来时，我是如此投入，以至于我只能屏住呼吸。

blocks.html_en:
for me it's often codebase decomposition.

it's hard to modularize a monolith without keeping large chunks in context at first pre-delegation; the orchestrator -- however you implement it -- needs to carry as much of the original thing as possible into context and big monoliths make this task heavier.

so, tl;dr : I use a lot of tokens re-writing legacy codebases that were constructed with very little CS training in some huge spaghetti fashion by random people around the world.

sentences.en: for me it's often codebase decomposition.it's hard to modularize a monolith without keeping large chunks in context at first pre-delegation; the orchestrator -- however you implement it -- needs to carry as much of the original thing as possible into context and big monoliths make this task heavier.so, tl;dr : I use a lot of tokens re-writing legacy codebases that were constructed with very little CS training in some huge spaghetti fashion by random people around the world.
sentences.zh: 对我来说，这通常是代码库分解。在第一次预授权时，如果不将大块内容保留在上下文中，就很难对整体进行模块化；编排器——无论你如何实现——需要将尽可能多的原始内容带入上下文中，而大型巨石使这项任务变得更加繁重。所以，tl;dr：我使用大量代币重写遗留代码库，这些代码库是由世界各地随机的人以某种巨大的意大利面条式的很少的 CS 培训构建的。

What plan are you on?

With Anthropic I run out of quota very fast on a Pro plan with long contexts.

Is long context primarily for Max tier or for API usage? Or are there special ways of working with long context on a Pro plan?

blocks.html_en: A typical suggestion is to make the agent create or update a .md file once in a while, so the agent would remember the new important things that have shown up since the last update. The problem is, how would the agent know which things are really important. If it knows that well, then /compact should work well too...

blocks.html_en: If you want stuff to be referenced regularly, why are you doing it in the conversation instead of just putting the documents in the directory and referring to them in AGENTS.md or whatever?

sentences.en: If you want stuff to be referenced regularly, why are you doing it in the conversation instead of just putting the documents in the directory and referring to them in AGENTS.md or whatever?

You can mad customize it if you're willing to set up like, oh my pi or whatever.

I didn't quite get it tuned up enough to be a daily driver but I'm basically sure it can be hotrodded however you want. It comes out of the box with like four different vendor hostile compact strategies, including compacting into images at the smallest size Claude can read, which the "coding is basically solved" geniuses conveniently leak the resolution heuristic out of their website along with all the other side channels they print for the MSS.

blocks.html_en:
OpenAI compaction is very different than Anthropic compaction, especially in 5.6

I use GLM5.2 and gpt-5.5 and 5.6 and never noticed any quality degradation near the limits of the context or due to compaction.

blocks.html_en:
>I use GLM5.2 and gpt-5.5 and 5.6 and never noticed any quality degradation near the limits of the context or due to compaction.

that's because you and your workloads probably fall into a bucket of users where compaction is tuned well.

but the reality is that those processes need to pick what to carry over, and that's a tough thing to get right for everyone, so for me compaction is a signal to restart the session or to tell the LLM to reference existing design docs lest we go off the rails.

large context limits is one of the things that make the open model competitors very attractive to me.

sentences.en: >I use GLM5.2 and gpt-5.5 and 5.6 and never noticed any quality degradation near the limits of the context or due to compaction.that's because you and your workloads probably fall into a bucket of users where compaction is tuned well.but the reality is that those processes need to pick what to carry over, and that's a tough thing to get right for everyone, so for me compaction is a signal to restart the session or to tell the LLM to reference existing design docs lest we go off the rails.large context limits is one of the things that make the open model competitors very attractive to me.
sentences.zh: >我使用 GLM5.2 和 gpt-5.5 和 5.6，从未注意到任何接近上下文限制或由于压缩而导致的质量下降。那是因为您和您的工作负载可能属于压缩调整良好的用户范围。但现实是这些流程需要选择要继承的内容，而这对每个人来说都是一件很难的事情，所以对我来说，压缩是重新启动会话或告诉 LLM 参考现有设计文档的信号，以免我们走掉偏离轨道。大的上下文限制是开放模型竞争对手对我非常有吸引力的原因之一。

> The lack of long context is the main reason that I still end up using Anthropic.

blocks.html_en:
I don't know if this is why they did it, but I think it is generally a mistake to go beyond this context size anyway.

I don't think people realize how much dumber the models get at larger contexts and how much more the token cost is. I never let claude get about 300k and in general I don't compact, I just divide up the work in chunks where I can fit it into 300k and I try to keep really clean and slim docs and modular code bases.

It's still kind of annoying, sometimes the larger context is useful for one time tasks, but I think if you are regularly going beyond 300k you are losing a lot and probably have a poorly designed code base.

blocks.html_en:
>> I don't think people realize how much dumber the models get at larger contexts and how much more the token cost is.

It does not match my experience that the model gets significantly dumber. It does get slower and more expensive, yes, but that's a sacrifice that needs to be made when working on anything complex.

My process involves having the main agent use subagents to explore what is needed for the given task. Then it writes a plan. Then it has the plan adversarially reviewed by more subagents and hardens it. After all is said and done, the 1M token window is 30-40% full. This flow would never work with 272k context, and in fact I've had to tone it down significantly for 5.6 Sol. Which, now that I think about it, probably explains why the results I get with it are inferior.

sentences.en: >> I don't think people realize how much dumber the models get at larger contexts and how much more the token cost is.It does not match my experience that the model gets significantly dumber.
sentences.zh: >> 我认为人们没有意识到模型在更大的上下文中变得多么愚蠢以及代币成本有多少。模型变得明显更加愚蠢，这与我的经验不符。

sentences.en: It does get slower and more expensive, yes, but that's a sacrifice that needs to be made when working on anything complex.My process involves having the main agent use subagents to explore what is needed for the given task.
sentences.zh: 是的，它确实变得更慢、更昂贵，但这是在处理任何复杂的事情时需要做出的牺牲。我的过程包括让主代理使用子代理来探索给定任务所需的内容。

sentences.en: Then it writes a plan.
sentences.zh: 然后它写了一个计划。

sentences.en: Then it has the plan adversarially reviewed by more subagents and hardens it.
sentences.zh: 然后，它让更多的子代理对计划进行对抗性审查并强化它。

sentences.en: After all is said and done, the 1M token window is 30-40% full.
sentences.zh: 归根结底，1M 代币窗口已满 30-40%。

sentences.en: This flow would never work with 272k context, and in fact I've had to tone it down significantly for 5.6 Sol.
sentences.zh: 这个流程永远不会在 272k 上下文中工作，事实上，我不得不为 5.6 Sol 大幅降低它。

sentences.en: Which, now that I think about it, probably explains why the results I get with it are inferior.
sentences.zh: 现在我想起来，这可能解释了为什么我用它得到的结果较差。

blocks.html_en:
>> I don't think people realize how much dumber the models get at larger contexts and how much more the token cost is.

It does not match my experience that the model gets significantly dumber. It does get slower and more expensive, yes, but that's a sacrifice that needs to be made when working on anything complex.

My process involves having the main agent use subagents to explore what is needed for the given task. Then it writes a plan. Then it has the plan adversarially reviewed by more subagents and hardens it. After all is said and done, the 1M token window is 30-40% full. This flow would never work with 272k context, and in fact I've had to tone it down significantly for 5.6 Sol. Which, now that I think about it, probably explains why the results I get with it are inferior.

blocks.html_en: The chart makes sense and is describing the cumulative cost of a trajectory. Cache tokens are created when a trajectory’s prefix is used more than once. A larger pre-compaction context window means that a greater number of cache tokens are used per turn, and a larger number of turns are completed before compaction runs. So you get a cumulative cost that grows quadratically until the compaction event.

sentences.en: The chart makes sense and is describing the cumulative cost of a trajectory.
sentences.zh: 该图表很有意义，并且描述了轨迹的累积成本。

sentences.en: Cache tokens are created when a trajectory’s prefix is used more than once.
sentences.zh: 当轨迹的前缀被多次使用时，就会创建缓存令牌。

sentences.en: A larger pre-compaction context window means that a greater number of cache tokens are used per turn, and a larger number of turns are completed before compaction runs.
sentences.zh: 更大的预压缩上下文窗口意味着每轮使用更多数量的缓存令牌，并且在压缩运行之前完成更多轮次。

sentences.en: So you get a cumulative cost that grows quadratically until the compaction event.
sentences.zh: 因此，您会得到一个累积成本，该成本呈二次方增长，直到发生压缩事件。

blocks.html_en: Not a fan of their context compaction and I feel like 1M-token context should minimum today. Each day I see how GPT 5.5 and 5.6 struggle a bit after each compaction before they get to the full speed, sometimes focusing too much on some older steering message that made it into the compacted context.

sentences.en: Not a fan of their context compaction and I feel like 1M-token context should minimum today.
sentences.zh: 不喜欢他们的上下文压缩，我觉得今天 1M 代币上下文应该最少。

sentences.en: Each day I see how GPT 5.5 and 5.6 struggle a bit after each compaction before they get to the full speed, sometimes focusing too much on some older steering message that made it into the compacted context.
sentences.zh: 每天，我都会看到 GPT 5.5 和 5.6 在每次压缩之后在全速运行之前会遇到一些困难，有时会过于关注一些旧的引导消息，这些消息会进入压缩上下文。

blocks.html_en: The improved token efficiency would only account for the output and reasoning tokens occupying context, right? What about the user messages and other context like file contents? I’m not sure how these measure up in a long conversation

sentences.en: What about the user messages and other context like file contents?
sentences.zh: 用户消息和其他上下文（例如文件内容）怎么样？

sentences.en: I’m not sure how these measure up in a long conversation
sentences.zh: 我不确定这些在长时间的谈话中表现如何

blocks.html_en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction like every other coding harness makes me scream. It fires randomly at 10-20%, so really you now only have guaranteed context of 272k * 80%. So many times I've had it work on a large codebase, it is almost done with a task, it needs maybe a 2000 token response, but it drops below 20%, it churns and churns and churns and then I see the dreaded ``Context compacted`` and scream. You can't go back in the history to the state of the conversation before the compaction. So then it has to investigate the codebase again gets to 20% and then auto-compacts. So it then has to read more, and then auto-compacts again, and then I'm out of tokens.

sentences.en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction like every other coding harness makes me scream.
sentences.zh: 事实上，没有办法像其他编码工具一样禁用自动压缩，这让我尖叫。

sentences.en: It fires randomly at 10-20%, so really you now only have guaranteed context of 272k * 80%.
sentences.zh: 它以 10-20% 随机触发，所以实际上您现在只能保证 272k * 80% 的上下文。

sentences.en: So many times I've had it work on a large codebase, it is almost done with a task, it needs maybe a 2000 token response, but it drops below 20%, it churns and churns and churns and then I see the dreaded ``Context compacted`` and scream.
sentences.zh: 很多次我让它在一个大型代码库上工作，它几乎完成了一个任务，它可能需要 2000 个令牌响应，但它下降到 20% 以下，它不断地搅动，然后我看到可怕的“上下文压缩”并尖叫。

sentences.en: You can't go back in the history to the state of the conversation before the compaction.
sentences.zh: 您无法在历史记录中返回到压缩之前的对话状态。

sentences.en: So then it has to investigate the codebase again gets to 20% and then auto-compacts.
sentences.zh: 因此，它必须再次调查代码库，达到 20%，然后自动压缩。

sentences.en: So it then has to read more, and then auto-compacts again, and then I'm out of tokens.
sentences.zh: 所以它必须读取更多内容，然后再次自动压缩，然后我就没有令牌了。

blocks.html_en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction like every other coding harness makes me scream. It fires randomly at 10-20%, so really you now only have guaranteed context of 272k * 80%. So many times I've had it work on a large codebase, it is almost done with a task, it needs maybe a 2000 token response, but it drops below 20%, it churns and churns and churns and then I see the dreaded ``Context compacted`` and scream. You can't go back in the history to the state of the conversation before the compaction. So then it has to investigate the codebase again gets to 20% and then auto-compacts. So it then has to read more, and then auto-compacts again, and then I'm out of tokens.

sentences.zh: 事实上，没有办法像其他编码工具一样禁用自动压缩，这让我尖叫。

sentences.en: So many times I've had it work on a large codebase, it is almost done with a task, it needs maybe a 2000 token response, but it drops below 20%, it churns and churns and churns and then I see the dreaded ``Context compacted`` and scream.
sentences.zh: 很多次我让它在一个大型代码库上工作，它几乎完成了一个任务，它可能需要 2000 个令牌响应，但它下降到 20% 以下，它不断地搅动，然后我看到可怕的“上下文压缩”并尖叫。

sentences.zh: 您无法在历史记录中返回到压缩之前的对话状态。

sentences.en: So then it has to investigate the codebase again gets to 20% and then auto-compacts.
sentences.zh: 因此，它必须再次调查代码库，达到 20%，然后自动压缩。

sentences.en: So it then has to read more, and then auto-compacts again, and then I'm out of tokens.
sentences.zh: 所以它必须读取更多内容，然后再次自动压缩，然后我就没有令牌了。

blocks.html_en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction like every other coding harness makes me scream. It fires randomly at 10-20%, so really you now only have guaranteed context of 272k * 80%. So many times I've had it work on a large codebase, it is almost done with a task, it needs maybe a 2000 token response, but it drops below 20%, it churns and churns and churns and then I see the dreaded ``Context compacted`` and scream. You can't go back in the history to the state of the conversation before the compaction. So then it has to investigate the codebase again gets to 20% and then auto-compacts. So it then has to read more, and then auto-compacts again, and then I'm out of tokens.

sentences.en: The fact there is no way to disable auto-compaction like every other coding harness makes me scream.
sentences.zh: 事实上，没有办法像其他编码工具一样禁用自动压缩，这让我尖叫。

sentences.en: It fires randomly at 10-20%, so really you now only have guaranteed context of 272k * 80%.
sentences.zh: 它以 10-20% 随机触发，所以实际上您现在只能保证 272k * 80% 的上下文。

sentences.en: So many times I've had it work on a large codebase, it is almost done with a task, it needs maybe a 2000 token response, but it drops below 20%, it churns and churns and churns and then I see the dreaded ``Context compacted`` and scream.
sentences.zh: 很多次我让它在一个大型代码库上工作，它几乎完成了一个任务，它可能需要 2000 个令牌响应，但它下降到 20% 以下，它不断地搅动，然后我看到可怕的“上下文压缩”并尖叫。

sentences.en: You can't go back in the history to the state of the conversation before the compaction.
sentences.zh: 您无法在历史记录中返回到压缩之前的对话状态。

sentences.en: So then it has to investigate the codebase again gets to 20% and then auto-compacts.
sentences.zh: 因此，它必须再次调查代码库，达到 20%，然后自动压缩。

sentences.en: So it then has to read more, and then auto-compacts again, and then I'm out of tokens.
sentences.zh: 所以它必须读取更多内容，然后再次自动压缩，然后我就没有令牌了。

blocks.html_en: Pi can do all the same that CC can. OSS is nice but the reason to use it is to control what is happening between me and the LLM. See: OP and many others for many reasons, bloated system prompts, the harness changing day to day, Anthropic shipping terrible and untested [features](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48947776) that don't appear in the changelog, Codex ultra subagents nuking usage, etc. You can tell which harnesses are vibe coded.

blocks.html_en:
I agree, but when it comes to the Pi defaults. I’ve spent a good deal of time (admittedly via vibe coding) writing extensions that completely alter the look/feel as well as functionality of Pi.

I view Pi as similar to NeoVim, both programs have kinda crappy defaults that nobody should really use but are incredibly extensible and customizable for power users willing to invest their time into them.

sentences.en: I agree, but when it comes to the Pi defaults.
sentences.zh: 我同意，但是当涉及到 Pi 默认值时。

sentences.en: I’ve spent a good deal of time (admittedly via vibe coding) writing extensions that completely alter the look/feel as well as functionality of Pi.I view Pi as similar to NeoVim, both programs have kinda crappy defaults that nobody should really use but are incredibly extensible and customizable for power users willing to invest their time into them.
sentences.zh: 我花了很多时间（诚然是通过 Vivi 编码）编写扩展程序，这些扩展程序完全改变了 Pi 的外观/感觉以及功能。我认为 Pi 与 NeoVim 类似，这两个程序都有一些蹩脚的默认设置，没有人应该真正使用，但对于愿意投入时间的高级用户来说，它们具有令人难以置信的可扩展性和可定制性。

blocks.html_en:
Pardon the plug, but I have built a tool that ingests the state of conversations to a local DB in realtime. It has both macOS and Linux clients.

When context compaction introduces a gap, I use the /total-recall skill to pull prior turns back into context and off it goes.

The tool is free for personal use and has a source available local cloud option to sync convo histories across multiple machines.

http://contextify.sh/docs

sentences.en: Pardon the plug, but I have built a tool that ingests the state of conversations to a local DB in realtime.
sentences.zh: 请原谅这个插件，但我已经构建了一个工具，可以将对话状态实时提取到本地数据库。

sentences.en: It has both macOS and Linux clients.When context compaction introduces a gap, I use the /total-recall skill to pull prior turns back into context and off it goes.The tool is free for personal use and has a source available local cloud option to sync convo histories across multiple machines.http://contextify.sh/docs
sentences.zh: 它有 macOS 和 Linux 客户端。当上下文压缩引入间隙时，我使用 /total-recall 技能将先前的回合拉回上下文并关闭它。该工具免费供个人使用，并且具有可用的源本地云选项，可以跨多台计算机同步 convo 历史记录。http://contextify.sh/docs

blocks.html_en:
Full agreement. I sincerely hope that reducing token is mostly a cost saving measure and not some roundabout way to get more token use. It still does not help. In my corp, bean counters rendered initially useful internal llm largely pointless as they severely limited context.

I swear there is a executive convention out there sharing worst practices.

sentences.en: Full agreement.
sentences.zh: 完全一致。

sentences.en: I sincerely hope that reducing token is mostly a cost saving measure and not some roundabout way to get more token use.
sentences.zh: 我真诚地希望减少代币主要是一种节省成本的措施，而不是某种迂回的方式来获得更多代币的使用。

sentences.en: It still does not help.
sentences.zh: 这仍然没有帮助。

sentences.en: In my corp, bean counters rendered initially useful internal llm largely pointless as they severely limited context.I swear there is a executive convention out there sharing worst practices.
sentences.zh: 在我的公司，精打细算使得最初有用的内部LLM在很大程度上毫无意义，因为它们严重限制了背景。我发誓有一个执行会议在那里分享最糟糕的做法。

blocks.html_en: Just use md files for working memory. No need for large context. LLMs get dumber as you put more in context, as it stretches their attention. Keeping context small is better for quality.

sentences.en: Just use md files for working memory.
sentences.zh: 只需使用 md 文件作为工作内存即可。

sentences.en: No need for large context.
sentences.zh: 不需要大的上下文。

sentences.en: LLMs get dumber as you put more in context, as it stretches their attention.
sentences.zh: 当你把更多的内容放在上下文中时，法学硕士会变得更愚蠢，因为这会分散他们的注意力。

sentences.en: Keeping context small is better for quality.
sentences.zh: 保持上下文较小更有利于质量。

blocks.html_en: Big context windows made everyone lazy about deciding what actually deserves to be in there... compaction makes it worse, it's lossy compression applied to everything at once so the details you actually needed probably get lost. I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problem... in my experience with conversational agents I clear the context every turn and reinject all the relevant info with a memory/context plugin I built myself, so small context stopped being a problem cos the model reads a few k of curated state instead of 200k of scrollback. Coding agents are harder and I haven't cracked that part, but in my opinion the real fix to the context problem is in this direction: a policy that keeps what's needed to finish the job or to do the next job and drops the rest. This can be achieved with a separate LLM specialized for exactly that.

sentences.en: Big context windows made everyone lazy about deciding what actually deserves to be in there...
sentences.zh: 大的上下文窗口让每个人都懒得决定里面到底应该放什么......

sentences.en: compaction makes it worse, it's lossy compression applied to everything at once so the details you actually needed probably get lost.
sentences.zh: 压缩使情况变得更糟，它是有损压缩一次应用于所有内容，因此您实际需要的细节可能会丢失。

sentences.en: I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problem...
sentences.zh: 我一直认为每次重新传输完整的聊天内容是一个根本问题......

sentences.en: in my experience with conversational agents I clear the context every turn and reinject all the relevant info with a memory/context plugin I built myself, so small context stopped being a problem cos the model reads a few k of curated state instead of 200k of scrollback.
sentences.zh: 根据我使用对话代理的经验，我每次都会清除上下文，并使用我自己构建的内存/上下文插件重新注入所有相关信息，因此小的上下文不再是问题，因为模型读取了几 k 的策划状态而不是 200k 的回滚。

sentences.en: Coding agents are harder and I haven't cracked that part, but in my opinion the real fix to the context problem is in this direction: a policy that keeps what's needed to finish the job or to do the next job and drops the rest.
sentences.zh: 编码代理更难，我还没有破解这部分，但在我看来，上下文问题的真正解决方案是在这个方向：保留完成工作或完成下一项工作所需的内容并放弃其余部分的策略。

sentences.en: This can be achieved with a separate LLM specialized for exactly that.
sentences.zh: 这可以通过专门针对此目的的单独法学硕士来实现。

blocks.html_en: Big context windows made everyone lazy about deciding what actually deserves to be in there... compaction makes it worse, it's lossy compression applied to everything at once so the details you actually needed probably get lost. I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problem... in my experience with conversational agents I clear the context every turn and reinject all the relevant info with a memory/context plugin I built myself, so small context stopped being a problem cos the model reads a few k of curated state instead of 200k of scrollback. Coding agents are harder and I haven't cracked that part, but in my opinion the real fix to the context problem is in this direction: a policy that keeps what's needed to finish the job or to do the next job and drops the rest. This can be achieved with a separate LLM specialized for exactly that.

blocks.html_en:
> I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problem

So did many NLP experts who worked on LSTM, GRU, etc. But transformers empirically won out and here we are.

It will be interesting to see if future model architectures need to take this into account again.

If humans are any indication, one thing we're still missing is efficient movement of information from short term to long term memory. Fine tuning in principle does something akin to this, but not efficiently.

sentences.en: > I always thought that retransmitting the full chat every turn is a foundamental problemSo did many NLP experts who worked on LSTM, GRU, etc. But transformers empirically won out and here we are.It will be interesting to see if future model architectures need to take this into account again.If humans are any indication, one thing we're still missing is efficient movement of information from short term to long term memory.
sentences.zh: > 我一直认为每回合重传完整的聊天内容是一个基本问题，许多研究 LSTM、GRU 等的 NLP 专家也是如此。但 Transformer 凭经验胜出了，我们就在这里。看看未来的模型架构是否需要再次考虑到这一点将会很有趣。如果人类有任何迹象的话，我们仍然缺少的一件事就是信息从短期记忆到长期记忆的有效移动。

sentences.en: Fine tuning in principle does something akin to this, but not efficiently.
sentences.zh: 原则上微调的作用与此类似，但效率不高。

blocks.html_en: I primarily use Opus for my day to day and I /clear often. 1M context sound great but it quickly degrades once you start hitting the 50% mark. I usually just /clear once I hit between 30 and 40 percent context and get much better results from the model. Compaction doesn’t really help much in my experience, starting fresh and having the model front load its context from scratch works much better IME. I have multiple skills with a pile of markdown docs organized by feature to assist in that first load by telling it where to find relevant information for the task. Works great.

sentences.en: I primarily use Opus for my day to day and I /clear often.
sentences.zh: 我主要在日常中使用 Opus，并且经常 /clear。

sentences.en: 1M context sound great but it quickly degrades once you start hitting the 50% mark.
sentences.zh: 1M 上下文听起来很棒，但一旦你开始达到 50% 的标准，它就会很快退化。

sentences.en: I usually just /clear once I hit between 30 and 40 percent context and get much better results from the model.
sentences.zh: 通常，一旦达到 30% 到 40% 的上下文，我就会 /clear 并从模型中获得更好的结果。

sentences.en: Compaction doesn’t really help much in my experience, starting fresh and having the model front load its context from scratch works much better IME.
sentences.zh: 根据我的经验，压缩并没有多大帮助，从头开始，让模型从头开始加载其上下文，IME 效果更好。

sentences.en: I have multiple skills with a pile of markdown docs organized by feature to assist in that first load by telling it where to find relevant information for the task.
sentences.zh: 我拥有多种技能，拥有一堆按功能组织的 Markdown 文档，通过告诉它在哪里可以找到任务的相关信息来协助第一次加载。

sentences.en: Works great.
sentences.zh: 效果很好。

blocks.html_en: I never felt that context size was any issue at all in codex. I don't know how their compaction works, but it just keeps on going as if it has no context limit at all. At least in my experience.

blocks.html_en:
> I never felt that context size was any issue at all in codex.

I'm guessing you just started using codex, it suffered greatly from "model context size exceeded" errors early on, where compaction couldn't even recover from it, these errors just stopped happening maybe some month(s) ago.

Nowadays it's a lot better though and I've don't get stuck in those anymore. Although I don't like the idea that they don't surface what goes into the "concise summary" afterwards, make it really hard to know if it actually got everything important or not.

In general, codex seems to be moving into the way of hiding as much from the user as possible, and it wouldn't surprise me if they eventually start encrypting the entire session logs just like they did with agent>sub-agent prompts recently. Sad though as it's easily the best harness+model combo available today out of the ones I've tried.

sentences.en: > I never felt that context size was any issue at all in codex.I'm guessing you just started using codex, it suffered greatly from "model context size exceeded" errors early on, where compaction couldn't even recover from it, these errors just stopped happening maybe some month(s) ago.Nowadays it's a lot better though and I've don't get stuck in those anymore.
sentences.zh: > 我从来没有觉得 codex 中的上下文大小有任何问题。我猜你刚刚开始使用 codex，它很早就遭受了“模型上下文大小超出”错误的困扰，压缩甚至无法从中恢复，这些错误可能在几个月前就停止发生了。现在情况好多了，我不再陷入这些错误了。

sentences.en: Although I don't like the idea that they don't surface what goes into the "concise summary" afterwards, make it really hard to know if it actually got everything important or not.In general, codex seems to be moving into the way of hiding as much from the user as possible, and it wouldn't surprise me if they eventually start encrypting the entire session logs just like they did with agent>sub-agent prompts recently.
sentences.zh: 虽然我不喜欢这样的想法，即他们没有在之后的“简明摘要”中显示内容，但很难知道它是否真的得到了所有重要的内容。总的来说，Codex 似乎正在转向尽可能向用户隐藏的方式，如果他们最终开始加密整个会话日志，就像他们最近对代理>子代理提示所做的那样，我不会感到惊讶。

sentences.en: Sad though as it's easily the best harness+model combo available today out of the ones I've tried.
sentences.zh: 但很遗憾，因为它无疑是我尝试过的目前最好的安全带+模型组合。

Nowadays it's a lot better though and I've don't get stuck in those anymore. Although I don't like the idea that they don't surface what goes into the "concise summary" afterwards, make it really hard to know if it actually got everything important or not.

Sad though as it's easily the best harness+model combo available today out of the ones I've tried.
